"Other earphone functions: to activate the phone voice assistant, press and hold until the first beep, then let go.<PIC>To activate the music app, press and hold until the first beep, then let go; the music app resumes playback where you left off, or you can tap again to start music playback.<PIC>To turn ambient awareness and ANC modes on or off, press the left earbud to cycle through the modes.<PIC>Low Latency Mode reduces Bluetooth audio delay for lip-sync and gaming; to turn it on or off, press and hold until the second beep, then let go.<PIC>", ["earphones_01", "earphones_02", "earphones_03", "Manual12_10"]

"Reset the earbuds only after they are disconnected from Bluetooth and removed from the charging case. For a factory reset, press and hold the multifunction control for 6 seconds; this erases all settings.<PIC>For a hardware reset, which restarts unresponsive earbuds, press and hold for 10 seconds, then press again to power on. Flashing red and blue lights indicate reset/status feedback; hardware reset is only needed when the earbuds lock up or do not respond.<PIC>", ["Manual12_11", "Manual12_12"]

"Earphone maintenance and care: clean the earphones with a soft cloth or paper towel only; do not use harsh chemicals or detergents, and make sure the earphones are dry before charging. When not in use, store them in a cool, dry place. Connect and disconnect charging cables carefully, and never tug or yank a cable while it is connected to the charging case. Do not expose the earphones to high heat or extreme cold. Recycle or dispose of them according to local laws and rules, and contact local recycling facilities or the manufacturer for more information. The listed sweat and dustproof rating is IP55; the battery type is lithium polymer; Bluetooth version is V5.", []

"The eReader device description is shown from three views. Front view: Home/ESC, Prev/Next Page, Navigation/Menu, Zoom in/out, Rotate, 3.5 mm earphone jack, USB port, Micro SD card reader, Play/Pause, Power, Volume up/down, reset on/off switch, speaker, and 7-inch TFT LCD display.<PIC>Navigation button view: the navigation/menu control is a four-way button for left, right, up, and down movement, and the menu/confirm action is handled through the M/navigation control.<PIC>Bottom view: the bottom-side interface layout is shown in the illustration.<PIC>", ["Manual13_0", "Manual13_1", "Manual13_2"]

"Main Menu shows the eReader's major functions: Browser History, eBook, Music, Video, Photo, Record, Explorer, and Settings. Select the desired function, then enter it.<PIC>Browser History lists recently browsed files. Use the navigation keys to select a book and press M; the reader opens the last page you read in that book.<PIC>If recently read files on the Micro SD card have been deleted, their records may remain in Browser History, but those files cannot be opened.", ["Manual13_3", "Manual13_4"]

"When you press M in eBook mode, the reader opens the reading menu with Page Jump, Save Mark, Load Mark, Del Mark, Browser Mode, Flip Time, Brightness, and Set Color.<PIC>From that menu you can jump to a page, save or load bookmarks, delete bookmarks, change browser mode, set automatic page-flip time, adjust brightness, or set the reading text color.<PIC>", ["eReader_08", "Manual13_5"]

"To listen to music, open the audio files list from the main menu, select the desired audio file, and press M to play. You can add audio by connecting the eReader to a computer with the USB cable, then dragging the desired audio files onto the device. The device supports MP3, WMA, WAV, ACC, and ECT formats, and can play audio while you read an eBook or browse photos or menus.<PIC>Use the Up/Down keys to adjust volume, Play/Pause to pause or continue, hold left/right to rewind or fast-forward, and press left/right briefly for previous or next audio. Press M in Music mode to adjust Repeat Mode, Play Mode, MS EQ, EQ Sel, or User EQ setup.<PIC>", ["Manual13_7", "Manual13_8"]

"Yes. To record voice, select Record from the main menu and press M to enter voice-record mode.<PIC>Press Play/Pause to start recording, press it again to pause, and press HOME to stop. When prompted after recording, choose YES or NOT with the M key to save or discard the recording.<PIC>To play a recording later, open Music from the main menu, enter the Music menu with M, choose Record, then select the recorded file and press M to play it.", ["Manual13_11", "Manual13_12"]

"To play video on the eReader, go to Video and press the M key to enter Video mode. The device supports AVI, RMVB, and MPEG2 video formats.<PIC>During video playback, press M to open options such as Subtitle Language, Time Play, Full Screen, and Brightness. Subtitle Language changes available subtitles, Time Play jumps to a selected time, Full Screen switches among Full screen, Normal, and Original display modes, and Brightness offers five levels.", ["Manual13_9"]

"Yes, the eReader can be used as a photo viewer. Go to Photo and press the M button to enter Photo mode; the device supports JPEG and BMP image formats. Use the left/right navigation keys to move to the previous or next photo, press Play/Pause to rotate the photo counterclockwise, and use the zoom keys to zoom in or out. Large photos may take longer to load or rotate than smaller files.<PIC>", ["Manual13_10"]

"Common eReader troubleshooting is handled by symptom. If it cannot power on, charge it until the battery indicator is full; if it still fails, contact the manufacturer. If it cannot charge, check whether the AC adapter or USB cable is defective. If there is no sound or noise in the earphones, adjust volume, try another earphone, and use better-quality music files. If files cannot be downloaded, check the USB cable, remaining memory, or required USB driver. If music files cannot play, the file format or resolution may be unsupported, so use a supported file or contact technical support.", []

"To connect the fax safely, connect it only to an AC power source within the range shown on the rating label; do not connect it to a DC power source or inverter, and ask a qualified electrician if you are unsure. Always make sure the plug is fully inserted, do not use a worn or frayed cord, and do not handle the plug with wet hands.<PIC>Before accessing the inside of the product, unplug the telephone line cord first, then the power cord from the AC outlet, and also disconnect any telephone/RJ-11 or Ethernet/RJ-45 cables as applicable.<PIC>If water, other liquids, or metal objects get inside, immediately unplug the product from the AC outlet and contact authorized service personnel.", ["Manual15_3", "Manual15_2"]

"To use the fax safely, read the Product Safety Guide before operation or maintenance and follow all warnings on the product. Use a grounded outlet, protect the power cord from damage, and unplug the product and contact authorized service if it is dropped, damaged, exposed to liquid, rain, or water, or does not operate normally. Do not use it during a thunderstorm, near water, or to report a gas leak.<PIC>Do not clean it with flammable sprays, alcohol, ammonia, or organic solvents.<PIC>Place it on a flat, stable surface, secure cords so they are not tripping hazards, wait until pages exit before picking them up, and keep fingers and foreign objects away from ink slots, trays, and illustrated pinch areas.<PIC>Additional pinch-area examples are shown in the illustration.<PIC>", ["Manual15_4", "Manual15_5", "Manual15_6", "Manual15_7"]

"CAUTION Wait until pages have exited the product before picking them up. Failure to do this may cause injury to your fingers by trapping them inside the machine. DO NOT put your hand or any foreign objects into the ink insertion slot. Doing this may cause injury. If the tray has been extended to hold large paper, position the product so that the tray does not protrude past the edge of the table or desk. If someone hits the tray, the product could fall and cause injury.<PIC>To prevent injuries, be careful not to put your fingers in the areas shown in the illustrations.<PIC>When changing the tray size from the large paper size to the original size, be careful not to pinch your fingers in the gaps or slots in the bottom of the tray. It may cause injury to you.", ["Manual15_6", "Manual15_7"]

"To prevent possible injuries, at least two people should lift the product. One person should hold the front of the product, and one person should hold the back, as shown in the illustration below. Carry the product by sliding your hands into the handhold indentations located on each side of the product. Be careful not to trap your fingers when you put the product down.<PIC>DO NOT carry the product by holding the areas shown in the illustrations. Doing this may cause the product to slip out of your hands and may result in injury.<PIC>(MFC-J5340DW/MFC-J5855DW) Carry the product by sliding your hands into the handhold indentations located on each side of the product.<PIC>When carrying the product, remove the trays if they have been expanded to hold large paper. The weight of the paper could cause the trays to fall and cause injury to you or others.<PIC>", ["fax_08", "Manual15_10", "Manual15_11", "Manual15_12"]

"Read this guide before attempting to operate the product, or before attempting any maintenance, and ensure that you follow all warnings and instructions marked on the product. Symbols and Conventions Used in the Documentation Prohibition icons indicate actions that must not be performed.<PIC>This icon indicates that flammable sprays must not be used.<PIC>This icon indicates that organic solvents such as alcohol, or other liquids must not be used.<PIC>Electrical Hazard icons alert you to possible electrical shock.<PIC>Fire Hazard icons alert you to the possibility of a fire.<PIC>This icon alerts you to the possibility of being injured by moving parts.<PIC>This icon alerts you to the areas that contain moving parts.", ["fax_01", "fax_02", "fax_03", "fax_04", "fax_05", "fax_06"]

"Industry Canada Compliance Statement (Canada only): this device complies with Industry Canada's licence-exempt RSS standards. Operation is subject to two conditions: the device may not cause interference, and it must accept any interference, including interference that may cause undesired operation. For indoor use only in the 5150 MHz to 5350 MHz band.", []

"To connect the regulator to the LP tank, secure the tank on the grill, turn all control knobs to OFF, and turn the LP tank off by rotating the OPD hand wheel clockwise to a full stop. Remove the protective cap from the LP tank valve, using the cap and strap supplied with the valve.<PIC>Do not use a POL transport plug, because it defeats the safety feature of the valve.<PIC>Hold the regulator straight, insert the nipple into the LP tank valve, and hand-tighten the coupling nut without cross-threading.<PIC>Turn the coupling nut clockwise to a full stop, then another one-half to three-quarters turn by hand only; do not use tools.", ["Manual19_16", "Manual19_17", "Manual19_18"]

"Leak testing valves, hose, and regulator: 1. Turn all grill control knobs to OFF. 2. Make sure the regulator is tightly connected to the LP tank. 3. Fully open the LP tank valve by turning the OPD hand wheel counterclockwise; if you hear a rushing sound, turn the gas off immediately because there is a major leak at the connection. 4. Brush a soap-and-water solution onto the areas shown in the picture.<PIC>5. If growing bubbles appear, close the LP tank valve immediately and retighten connections. If leaks cannot be stopped, do not try to repair them; call for replacement parts. 6. Always close the LP tank valve after the leak test by turning the hand wheel clockwise.<PIC>", ["Manual19_20", "Manual19_21"]

"For indirect cooking, poultry and large cuts of meat cook slowly by heat circulating from selected burners, without direct contact with flame. This reduces flare-ups because fat and juices do not drip onto a direct flame.<PIC>Always cook with the lid closed; in cold or windy weather, increase the temperature setting as needed to maintain sufficient cooking temperature.<PIC>", ["Manual19_36", "Manual19_37"]

"The first three assembly steps are: 1. Attach the two locking casters at the rear of the bottom shelf and the two fixed casters at the front using the supplied wrench.<PIC>2. Attach the side panels to the bottom shelf using three 1/4-20 x 1/2 inch screws and 7 mm lock washers per panel; the panel with the hole must be on the left side.<PIC>3. Attach the light adapter to the back panel, then place the lower back panel between the side panels and secure it to the side panels and bottom shelf as shown.<PIC>", ["Manual19_49", "Manual19_50", "Manual19_51"]

"Safety Tips: before opening the LP tank valve, check the coupling nut for tightness. When the grill is not in use, turn off all control knobs and the LP tank valve. Never move the grill while it is operating or still hot. Use long-handled barbecue utensils and oven mitts to avoid burns and splatters. The maximum load for shelves is 10 lb. Keep the grease tray installed in the back-panel opening and empty it after each use only after the grill has cooled. If grease or other hot material drips onto the valve, hose, or regulator, turn off the gas supply, correct the cause, clean and inspect those parts, and perform a leak test.<PIC>If the regulator frosts, turn off the grill and LP tank valve immediately and return the tank to the supplier.<PIC>", ["Manual19_24", "Manual19_25"]

"Identification numbers: record the Primary Identification (PRI-ID) number, Hull Identification Number (HIN), and engine serial number for ordering spare parts. Keep a separate record in case the watercraft is stolen. The PRI-ID number is stamped on a label inside the engine compartment.<PIC>The PRI-ID location is shown on the watercraft.<PIC>The HIN is stamped on a plate attached to the hull on the aft starboard (right) side.<PIC>The HIN location is shown on the hull.<PIC>The engine serial number is stamped on a label attached to the engine unit.<PIC>The engine serial number location is shown on the engine.<PIC>", ["Manual20_0", "jetski_01", "Manual20_1", "jetski_02", "Manual20_2", "jetski_03"]

"Cruising limitations: constantly scan for people, objects, and other watercraft, and be alert for conditions that limit visibility or block your view.<PIC>Operate defensively at safe speeds and keep a safe distance. Do not follow directly behind other boats, spray people, make sharp maneuvers that others cannot predict, or operate in areas with submerged objects or shallow water. Ride within your limits and avoid aggressive maneuvers, rough water, bad weather, or poor visibility, because these increase the risk of loss of control, ejection, collision, injury, or death.<PIC>Check forecasts and the current weather before setting out.<PIC>", ["Manual20_16", "Manual20_17", "Manual20_18"]

"Cruising limitations: constantly scan for people, objects, and other watercraft, and be alert for conditions that limit visibility or block your view.<PIC>Operate defensively at safe speeds and keep a safe distance. Do not follow directly behind other boats, spray people, make sharp maneuvers that others cannot predict, or operate in areas with submerged objects or shallow water. Ride within your limits and avoid aggressive maneuvers, rough water, bad weather, or poor visibility, because these increase the risk of loss of control, ejection, collision, injury, or death.<PIC>Check forecasts and the current weather before setting out.<PIC>", ["Manual20_16", "Manual20_17", "Manual20_18"]

"Operation requirements: all riders must wear a Coast Guard-approved personal flotation device suitable for personal watercraft use. All riders must also wear a wetsuit bottom or equivalent protective clothing, because normal swimwear does not adequately protect against forceful water entry near the jet thrust nozzle; the PFD and wetsuit-bottom requirements are shown in the figure.<PIC>Eye protection is recommended for wind, water, and glare, and footwear and gloves are also recommended. Decide whether to wear a Snell- or DOT-standard helmet for recreational riding after considering both the protection benefits and the water-entry risks. Never operate the watercraft after consuming alcohol or taking drugs.<PIC>Before operating, perform the listed pre-operation checks. While the watercraft is moving, the operator and passenger should keep both feet in the footwell; do not carry a child whose feet cannot reach the footwell, and the passenger should firmly hold on to the operator as shown.<PIC>", ["Manual20_19", "Manual20_20", "Manual20_21"]

"Watercraft characteristics: jet thrust turns the watercraft, so releasing the throttle greatly reduces steering ability above trolling speed. After the engine slows, the watercraft will not respond to handlebar input until you open the throttle again or reach trolling speed, so practice turning in open water. Before removing debris or weeds from the jet intake, stop the engine and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch; the clip and switch are shown in the figure.<PIC>This craft is water-jet propelled and has no neutral position. Whenever the engine is running, jet thrust can produce some movement. Keep away from the intake grate while the engine is on, and keep long hair, loose clothing, straps, and similar items away from that area; the intake-grate location is shown in the figure.<PIC>", ["Manual20_24", "Manual20_25"]

"When encountering other vessels, follow the Steering Rules to avoid collision. The three main situations are meeting head-on, crossing another vessel's path, and overtaking or being overtaken.<PIC>In the rule diagram, your watercraft is in the center. You must give way to vessels in the white area, while vessels in the shaded area must yield to you. In a meeting situation, both vessels should alter course to avoid each other.", ["Manual20_26"]

"To remove and install the seat, use the seat latch at the rear of the seat.<PIC>To remove it, pull the seat latch up and pull the seat off.<PIC>To install it, insert the projection on the front of the seat into the stay on the deck, then push the rear of the seat down until it locks securely.<PIC>Make sure the seat is secure before operating the watercraft.", ["Manual20_35", "Manual20_36", "Manual20_37"]

"To open the hood in daily use, push the hood latch down and lift the hood upward.<PIC>To close it, push the hood down until it locks in place.<PIC>After closing, always check that the hood is properly secured before operating the watercraft, because an unsecured hood can open or move while riding.", ["Manual20_38", "Manual20_39"]

"Fuel tank filler cap: turn the fuel tank filler cap counterclockwise to remove it, and make sure it is secured before operating the watercraft.<PIC>Oil tank filler cap: turn the oil tank filler cap counterclockwise to remove it, and make sure it is secured before operating the watercraft.<PIC>", ["Manual20_40", "Manual20_41"]

"There are two engine stop controls. Engine stop switch: push the red button to stop the engine normally.<PIC>Engine shut-off switch: insert the clip on the end of the engine shut-off cord under the black switch before operation; the engine stops automatically when the clip is removed, such as if the operator falls off.<PIC>Always attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist and the clip to the shut-off switch before starting. Remove the clip when the engine is not running to prevent accidental starting or unauthorized use.<PIC>", ["Manual20_43", "Manual20_44", "Manual20_45"]

"Choke lever: starting a cold engine requires a richer air-fuel mixture. Move the lever in the ON direction to turn the choke on, and move it back to turn the choke off.<PIC>Throttle lever: squeeze the throttle lever to increase engine speed; release it to decrease engine speed or return to idle.<PIC>", ["Manual20_46", "Manual20_47"]

"The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector is on the left handlebar grip and adjusts the watercraft trim angle by changing the jet thrust nozzle angle. It has five positions: two bow-down positions, neutral, and two bow-up positions. To change the trim angle, reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less, squeeze the shift lock lever, turn the QSTS selector to the desired position, and release the lever to lock it.<PIC>Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating at full throttle, or the QSTS may be damaged.<PIC>", ["Manual20_51", "Manual20_52"]

"Fuel meter: this meter is used as a convenient reference for checking fuel level while riding. It has eight segments showing the approximate fuel remaining in the tank; the segment indication can vary with operating conditions.<PIC>Hour meter: this meter helps you follow the maintenance schedule by showing total engine-operation hours since the watercraft was new. The elapsed time is retained even if the battery terminals are disconnected.<PIC>", ["Manual20_58", "Manual20_59"]

"Fuel filter inspection: this watercraft uses a one-piece disposable fuel filter. Replace it after the initial 10 hours or first month, and then every 200 hours or 24 months, or whenever water is found in the filter. Have a Yamaha dealer replace it if necessary.<PIC>Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself, because an incorrectly installed filter can leak gasoline and may cause fire or explosion. Fuel tank inspection: check the fuel tank for leakage or water in the tank. If water is found in the fuel system or the tank needs cleaning, have a Yamaha dealer service the watercraft.<PIC>", ["Manual20_81", "Manual20_82"]

"To adjust the adjustable sponsons, remove the bolts on both sponsons.<PIC>Remove both sponsons, install them in the desired position, and make sure both sides are installed at the same level. Then tighten the bolts on both sponsons to the specified torque of 18 N.m (1.8 kgf.m, 13 ft.lb).<PIC>", ["Manual20_83", "Manual20_84"]

"To clean the jet intake and impeller, first beach the watercraft and check the intake and impeller if weeds or debris cause cavitation, reduced jet thrust, or engine overheating. Always stop the engine before beaching the watercraft.<PIC>Before removing debris, shut the engine off and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch; rotating jet-pump parts can cause severe injury or death. Turn the watercraft onto its port side on a clean cloth or carpet, support the bow so the handlebars are not damaged, then remove weeds or debris from around the drive shaft, impeller, pump housing, and jet thrust nozzle.<PIC>If debris is difficult to remove, consult a Yamaha dealer.", ["Manual20_85", "Manual20_86"]

"Starting in shallow water: do not start the engine in less than 60 cm (2 ft) of water; move to water deep enough, then board from the side or rear. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, install the clip to the shut-off switch, grip the handlebars, place both feet in the footwell, start the engine, and start off.<PIC>Starting from a dock: board from the side, attach the shut-off cord and clip, push the watercraft away from the dock, grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet in the footwell, start the engine, and start off.<PIC>", ["Manual20_88", "Manual20_89"]

"Boarding with a passenger in deep water: do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated with both feet on the floor of the footwell and holding on to the operator. 1. The operator climbs aboard as described for boarding alone, sits astride the seat, attaches the engine shut-off cord to the wrist, and installs the clip to the shut-off switch. 2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft, get on board, and sit on the seat.<PIC>3. Both passenger and operator should balance the watercraft. The operator checks that the passenger's feet are on the floor of the footwell and that the passenger is securely holding on. 4. Start the engine, look in all directions, and accelerate to planing speed.<PIC>Before starting off, make sure there are no boats, obstructions, or swimmers around you.<PIC>", ["Manual20_90", "Manual20_91", "Manual20_92"]

"If it is difficult to balance the watercraft at a standstill, first note that the total operator, passenger, and cargo weight must not exceed 160 kg (353 lb). 1. The passenger steadies the watercraft while the operator boards; the operator pulls onto the platform into a seating position and balances the craft.<PIC>2. Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, install the clip on the engine shut-off switch, start the engine, and keep it at idle. 3. The passenger pulls onto the platform, kneels down, then crawls onto the seat as the watercraft accelerates.<PIC>4. Accelerate to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.<PIC>", ["jetski_06", "jetski_07", "jetski_08"]

"This watercraft is designed to carry the operator and one passenger. Never exceed the maximum load limit or allow more than 2 persons to ride the watercraft at one time. Maximum load: 160 kg (353 lb). Load is the total weight of cargo, operator and passenger.<PIC>", ["Manual20_15"]

"The base station overview shows one primary control button. A short press is used to find or page the registered handsets, which helps locate a handset around the home. Pressing and holding the same button puts the base station into handset registration mode so a handset can be registered to the base.<PIC>So, for normal daily use it is mainly a paging/find button, while a long press is for registration. If the handset cannot be found or needs registration, this button is the base-station control to start that process.", ["Manual22_18"]

"To connect the base station, first confirm that the power supply voltage matches the value printed on the back or underside of the phone, and use only the supplied power adapter.<PIC>If the phone line uses DSL service, install a DSL filter between the telephone line cord and the power socket to avoid noise and caller-ID problems. Connect the power adapter to the DC input jack on the bottom of the base station and to the wall power socket, then connect the line cord to the telephone socket on the base station and the telephone wall socket.<PIC>", ["Manual22_21", "Manual22_22"]

"To install the handset, note that the batteries are already pre-installed. Before charging or using the handset, pull the battery tape out from the battery door.<PIC>After removing the tape, place the handset on the base station or charger so the batteries can charge normally before first use.", ["Manual22_23"]

"To check the battery level, look at the battery icon on the handset display; it shows the current battery level.<PIC>If the batteries are empty, the handset turns off. During a call, warning tones sound when the batteries are almost empty, and the call disconnects after the warning.<PIC>", ["Manual22_28", "Manual22_29"]

"My handset is in searching status, what do I do? Make sure that the base station has power supply. Register the handset to the base station. Move the handset closer to the base station. If these solutions do not help, disconnect the power supply from both the handset and base station, then try again after 1 minute.<PIC>", ["Manual22_60"]

"To set the handset LED indicator behavior, select [Menu] > [Phone setup] > [LED status] > [Events status] or [Charge status], then press [OK] to confirm. The setting is saved, and the table shows the current status for each LED indicator behavior on the handset.<PIC>", ["Manual22_40"]

"The base-station LED table maps each LED behavior to message status. A steady-on LED means there is no new message and memory is not full, the handset is switched on, or the base is in paging mode. A flashing LED can mean there is a new message, an incoming call, recording of an incoming message or announcement, message playback, or remote access/listening from the handset. Fast flashing means there is no new message but memory is full, or the base station is in registration mode.<PIC>", ["Manual22_46"]

"Lowering the Roll Bar Important: Lower the roll bar only when absolutely necessary. 1. Remove the hairpin cotters and remove the 2 pins ( Figure 9 ). 2. Lower the roll bar to the down position ( Figure 8 ). Note: There are 2 down positions; refer to Figure 8.<PIC>Figure 8 3. Install the 2 pins and secure them with the hairpin cotters ( Figure 9 ).", ["Manual23_32"]

"Rear-shock assemblies, suspension-system models only: the suspension system can be adjusted for a smoother and more comfortable ride. Move the rear two shock assemblies to the position that feels most comfortable. The slots have detent positions for reference, but the assemblies can be positioned anywhere in the slot, not only at the detents.<PIC>Always keep the left and right rear-shock assemblies in the same positions, then adjust them as shown with the seat-suspension knob.<PIC>", ["Manual23_37", "Manual23_38"]

"Adjusting the Height of Cut For Machines with an Electric Deck Lift 1. Push up on the deck-lift switch ( Figure 31 ).<PIC>Figure 31 1. Push up to raise the deck. 2. Push down to lower the deck. 2. Select a hole in the height-of-cut bracket corresponding to the height of cut desired, and insert the pin ( Figure 32 ).<PIC>Figure 32 1. Height-of-cut pin 2. Height-of-cut bracket", ["Manual23_55", "Manual23_56"]

"Loading the Machine: loading onto a trailer or truck can cause tip-over, so use extreme caution on the ramp. Back the machine up the ramp and drive it forward down the ramp; avoid sudden acceleration or deceleration. 1. Connect the trailer to the towing vehicle and connect safety chains; connect trailer brakes and lights if applicable. 2. Lower the ramp, keeping the angle between the ramp and ground at 15 degrees or less.<PIC>3. Back the machine up the ramp.<PIC>4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, engage the parking brake, and tie down the machine near the front caster wheels and rear bumper with straps, chains, cable, or ropes.<PIC>", ["Manual23_63", "Manual23_64", "Manual23_65"]

"To unload the lawn mower from a trailer or truck, use a ramp and keep the ramp angle at 15 degrees or less.<PIC>Drive the machine forward down the ramp, keeping control and avoiding sudden acceleration or deceleration.<PIC>The related loading instructions warn that ramp work can cause tip-over, so the trailer should be connected securely and the operator should move slowly and deliberately. Do not rush the descent; the key points are a shallow ramp angle, forward travel down the ramp, and steady control.", ["Manual23_63", "Manual23_64"]

"Changing the engine oil: 1. Start the engine and let it run for 5 minutes so the oil drains better. 2. Park with the drain side slightly lower, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop. 3. Drain the oil from the engine (Figure 49).<PIC>4. Slowly pour about 80 percent of the specified oil into the filler tube, then add more oil until it reaches the Full mark (Figure 50).<PIC>5. Start the engine, drive to a flat area, and check the oil level again.", ["Manual23_75", "Manual23_76"]

"Removing the Filters 1. Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake. 2. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position. 3. Release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body ( Figure 46 ).<PIC>Figure 46 1. Air-cleaner body 4. Air-cleaner cover 2. Primary filter 5. Safety filter 3. Latch 4. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air. 5. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body ( Figure 46 ). Note: Avoid knocking the filter into the side of the body. 6. Remove the safety filter only to replace it.", ["Manual23_72"]

"Replacing the Mower Belt: 1. Park on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop. 2. Lower the mower to the 76 mm (3 inch) height of cut. 3. Remove the belt covers (Figure 73).<PIC>4. Use a 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole in the idler arm to remove tension on the idler spring; remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and clutch pulley, remove the belt guide, and remove the existing belt. 5. Install the new belt around the mower pulleys and clutch pulley under the engine (Figure 74).<PIC>6. Reinstall the belt guide, use the ratchet to install the idler spring with spring ends seated in the anchor grooves, and install the belt covers (Figure 75).<PIC>", ["Manual23_99", "Manual23_100", "Manual23_101"]

"Control Set-Up lets you change the microwave's default beep sound, clock display, display scroll speed, and defrost-weight unit. The setup chart shows the available choices: sound on/off, clock on/off, slow/normal/fast scroll speed, and pounds or kilograms for defrost weight.<PIC>For example, to change the defrost weight mode from pounds to kilograms, touch Control Set-Up, touch number 4 for LBS/KG choice, then touch number 2 for KG.<PIC>", ["Manual24_9", "Manual24_10"]

"Light Timer lets you set the microwave's low light to turn on and off automatically at the same times every day until the setting is reset. For example, to turn it on at 2:00 AM and off at 7:00 AM: touch Light Timer, choose AM/PM, enter 2-0-0, touch Light Timer again, choose AM/PM, enter 7-0-0, then touch Start.<PIC>To reset the on/off time, repeat the setting steps. To cancel the Light Timer, touch Light Timer, then touch Stop/Clear; if the timer is operating, touch the Light HI/LO/Off pad.<PIC>", ["Manual24_11", "Manual24_12"]

"Favorite Recipe stores one cooking instruction in memory so it can be recalled quickly. To program a 2-minute cooking recipe, touch Favorite Recipe, enter the cook time by pressing 2-0-0, then touch Start; the display begins counting down.<PIC>To recall the custom recipe later, touch Favorite Recipe and then touch Start.<PIC>When cooking is finished, four beeps sound and END appears; the power level defaults to Hi but can be modified.", ["Manual24_16", "Manual24_17"]

"REHEAT(SENSOR) Reheat lets you heat foods without needing to program times and Power Levels. Reheat has preset Power Levels for 3 categories: Casserole,Dinner Plate and Soup/Sauce<PIC>Example: To reheat Casserole<PIC>·When the cook time is over, you will hear four beeps and ENDwill display. ·Recommended amounts:<PIC>", ["Manual24_29", "Manual24_30", "Manual24_31"]

"Auto Defrost: the microwave has three preset defrost sequences, and Auto Defrost is the recommended way to defrost frozen foods.<PIC>For example, to defrost 1.2 lb of ground beef, follow the Auto Defrost example shown in the display sequence.<PIC>When START is touched, the display changes to a defrost-time countdown. The oven beeps during the cycle; open the door, turn, separate, or rearrange the food, remove thawed portions, then return frozen portions to the oven and touch START to resume.", ["Manual24_32", "Manual24_33"]

"Clean the grease filter often, at least once a month. To remove it, slide the filter to the side, pull it downward, then push it to the other side so it drops out.<PIC>Wash the filter in a dishwasher or soak it in hot water with mild detergent; rinse well and shake it dry. Do not use ammonia, and note that aluminum may darken in a dishwasher.<PIC>To reinstall, slide the filter into the side slot, then push it up and toward the oven center until it locks.<PIC>", ["Manual24_47", "Manual24_48", "Manual24_49"]

"Charcoal filter replacement: if the oven is vented inside, replace the charcoal filter every 6 to 12 months, or more often if necessary; the charcoal filter cannot be cleaned. 1. Unplug the oven or turn off power at the main power supply. 2. Remove the two vent grill mounting screws.<PIC>3. Tip the grill forward, lift it out, and remove the old filter.<PIC>4. Slide a new charcoal filter into place; it should rest at the angle shown.<PIC>5. Slide the bottom of the grill into place, push the top until it snaps into place, replace the mounting screws, turn the power back on, and set the clock.", ["Manual24_50", "Manual24_51", "Manual24_52"]

"To replace the oven light, unplug the oven or turn off power at the main supply. Remove the two middle vent-cover mounting screws, tip the cover forward, and lift it out.<PIC>Remove the bulb holder.<PIC>Lift up the bulb holder, replace the bulb with a 30 or 40 watt appliance bulb, then reinstall the holder.<PIC>Slide the bottom of the vent cover into place, push the top until it snaps in, replace the mounting screws, restore power, and plug in the oven or reset the clock as needed.", ["Manual24_54", "Manual24_55", "Manual24_56"]

"PCI Express 3.0 x16 slots: the motherboard has three PCI Express 3.0 x16 slots that support PCI Express 3.0 x16 graphics cards.<PIC>In single VGA-card mode, use the gray PCIe 3.0 x16_1 slot for better graphics-card performance. Provide sufficient power when running some modes, and connect a chassis fan to CHA_FAN1/2/3 when using multiple graphics cards for a better thermal environment.", ["Manual25_31"]

"The motherboard jumpers include Clear RTC RAM (2-pin CLRTC), which clears the CMOS Real Time Clock memory containing date, time, and system setup parameters such as passwords.<PIC>To erase RTC RAM, turn off the computer and unplug the power cord, short the two pins with a metal object such as a screwdriver, then plug in the power cord and turn on the computer. Hold Delete during boot to enter BIOS and re-enter the setup data. If this does not work, remove the onboard battery and short the pins again, then reinstall the battery.<PIC>For a hang caused by overclocking, you normally do not need to clear RTC; reboot and let CPU Parameter Recall reset settings to default.", ["Manual25_33", "Manual25_34"]

"Rear panel connectors include PS/2 mouse/keyboard, DisplayPort, VGA, LAN (RJ-45), analog audio ports, optical S/PDIF, USB 3.1 Type-A, USB 3.1 Type-C, USB 3.0, DVI-D, HDMI, and USB 2.0 ports.<PIC>The LAN port supports Gigabit LAN connection through a network hub.<PIC>Its LED indications are shown in the manual.<PIC>The orange, black, light-blue, lime, and pink audio jacks are used for center/subwoofer, rear speaker out, line in, line out/front speaker out, and microphone, depending on the 2, 4.1, 5.1, or 7.1-channel configuration.<PIC>", ["Manual25_35", "Manual25_36", "Manual25_37", "Manual25_38"]

"Onboard LED settings include the Standby Power LED and Model name LEDs. The Standby Power LED lights when the system is on, in sleep mode, or in soft-off mode, reminding you to shut down and unplug the power cable before changing motherboard components.<PIC>The Model name LEDs are five small LEDs below the printed model name and can light in different modes.<PIC>You can turn them off or change their modes from BIOS at Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Model Name LED Lighting, or through the LED Control app in Ai Suite 3.<PIC>", ["Manual25_54", "Manual25_55", "Manual25_56"]

"EZ Flash 3 updates the BIOS without an OS-based utility. Before updating, load BIOS default settings for compatibility and stability, and check the Internet connection if updating online.<PIC>To update, enter Advanced Mode in BIOS, open Tool > EZ Flash 3 Utility, and press Enter. For USB update, insert a FAT32/16 single-partition USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file, select by USB, choose the drive and BIOS file, then start the update. For Internet update, select by Internet, choose the connection method, and follow the on-screen instructions. Reboot after the update finishes, and do not shut down or reset the system during the update.", ["Manual25_61"]

"To create RAID, open EZ Tuning Wizard from BIOS by pressing F11 or clicking its icon, then click RAID and choose Yes to enable RAID. Before continuing, make sure the HDDs do not already contain RAID volumes and are connected to Intel SATA connectors. Select the available HDDs and click Next.<PIC>Choose the storage type: Easy Backup or Super Speed, then click Next.<PIC>For Easy Backup, select RAID1 or RAID10; RAID10 is available only when four HDDs are connected.<PIC>For Super Speed, select RAID0 or RAID5, then continue.<PIC>After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to create it, then click Yes and OK to exit setup and reset the system.", ["Manual25_72", "Manual25_73", "Manual25_74", "Manual25_75"]

"Method 1 uses a SATA optical drive and a USB device to load USB 3.0 drivers from the support DVD while installing OS 7. Required items are the support DVD, OS 7 installation source, one SATA ODD, and one USB ODD or storage device; a USB storage device should be 8 GB or larger and formatted before use. Insert the OS 7 installation DVD into a USB ODD, or copy all OS 7 installation files to a USB storage device, then connect it to the 100 series platform. Insert the support DVD into the SATA ODD, power on, and press F8 during POST to enter the boot screen and select the USB device.<PIC>The USB 3.0 driver loads automatically during installation startup; when the driver is loaded correctly, the Setup is starting screen appears. Then follow the on-screen instructions to complete OS 7 installation.<PIC>", ["Manual25_87", "Manual25_88"]

"To secure the motherboard to the chassis, place screws into the nine screw holes indicated by circles in the diagram.<PIC>Orient the board so the side marked in the illustration faces the rear of the chassis. Tighten the screws enough to hold the board securely, but do not over-tighten them because that can damage the motherboard.", ["Manual25_6"]

"CPU installation preparation: this motherboard uses a surface-mount LGA1151 socket for 6th Generation processors.<PIC>Unplug all power cables before installing the CPU. Install only an LGA1151 CPU; do not install LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 CPUs in this socket. Before installation, check that the PnP cap is on the socket and that socket contacts are not bent; contact the retailer if the cap, contacts, or motherboard components are damaged. Keep the cap after installing the motherboard because RMA service requires the cap on the LGA1151 socket.", ["Manual25_9"]

"System memory: this motherboard has four DDR4 DIMM sockets. DDR4 modules are notched differently from DDR, DDR2, and DDR3 modules, so do not install older memory types in the DDR4 slot. DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to protect the CPU.<PIC>You may install 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB unbuffered non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs; use the recommended memory population shown in the manual.<PIC>Different memory sizes may be installed in Channel A and Channel B; the system maps the lower-sized channel for dual-channel operation and any excess from the larger channel for single-channel operation.", ["Manual25_18", "Manual25_19"]

"1. Serial port connector (10-1 pin COM) This connector is for a serial (COM) port. Connect the serial port module cable to this connector, then install the module to a slot opening at the back of the system chassis.<PIC>Serial port (COM) connector The COM module is purchased separately.", ["Manual25_39"]

"2. TPM connector (14-1 pin TPM) This connector supports a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) system, which securely store keys, digital certificates, passwords and data. A TPM system also helps enhance the network security, protects digital identities, and ensures platform integrity.<PIC>TPM connector", ["Manual25_40"]

"The Thermal Sensor connector, labeled T_SENSOR and using a 2-pin header, is for connecting a thermistor cable. With that cable installed, the motherboard can monitor the temperature of critical motherboard components or connected devices selected by the user.<PIC>It is therefore a monitoring connector, not a power connector; connect only the appropriate thermal sensor cable. Use it when you need an additional temperature reading for thermal monitoring, especially around components whose temperature is important to system stability.", ["Manual25_43"]

"Quick Release (QR or QPR) Stops cooking quickly and prevents overcooking. Perfect for quick-cooking vegetables and delicate seafood! What to do Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.<PIC>What to expect When pressed down, a jet of steam ejects through the top of the steam release valve. This is normal! When pressure has been fully released, the float valve drops into the lid.<PIC>", ["multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01", "multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02"]

"The pressure cooking lid is used for Pressure Cook, Sauté, Slow Cook, Steam, and Sous Vide programs.<PIC>To remove it, grip the lid handle, turn the lid counterclockwise until the lid symbol aligns with the cooker-base symbol, then lift the lid up and off toward your body.<PIC>To close it, align the lid symbol with the cooker-base symbol, lower the lid onto the track, and turn it clockwise until the symbols align in the closed position.<PIC>", ["Manual30_11", "Manual30_12", "Manual30_13"]

"The float valve shows whether the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer is pressurized. When the float valve has popped up above the lid surface, the cooker is pressurized and the lid must not be forced open.<PIC>When the float valve has dropped and the top is flush with the lid, the cooker is depressurized.<PIC>The float valve and silicone cap must be installed before use because they work together to seal pressurized steam; do not touch the float valve during operation.", ["Manual30_17", "Manual30_18"]

"The anti-block shield is part of the pressure-cooking lid. It helps prevent food particles from coming up through the steam release pipe and assists with pressure regulation. Because it is integral to product safety, it must be in place for pressure cooking.<PIC>", ["Manual30_19"]

"To set the steam release valve, place it loosely on the steam release pipe as shown; it is designed to sit loose, not lock rigidly in place. During pressure release, steam ejects from the top of this valve, so keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from it.<PIC>Do not cover or block the steam release valve in any way, because it is an essential safety part for pressure cooking.", ["Manual30_15"]

"To set the anti-block shield, first remove it for cleaning by gripping the lid like a steering wheel and pressing firmly on the side of the shield with your thumbs until it pops off the prongs underneath.<PIC>To reinstall it, place the anti-block shield over the prongs and press down until it snaps into position. Do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed.<PIC>", ["Manual30_34", "Manual30_35"]

"For the float valve and silicone cap, remove the float valve by placing one finger on the flat top of the float valve, turning the lid over, detaching the silicone cap from the bottom side, and lifting the float valve out from the top of the lid. Do not discard either part.<PIC>To install it, drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float-valve hole on top of the lid, hold the flat top with one finger, turn the lid over, and firmly attach the silicone cap to the bottom of the float valve. Do not operate the pressure cooker unless the float valve and silicone cap are properly installed in the pressure-cooking lid.<PIC>", ["Manual30_36", "Manual30_37"]

"The condensation collector sits at the back of the cooker base and catches overflow from the condensation rim. It should be installed before cooking, then emptied and rinsed after each use. To install it, align the collector grooves over the tabs on the back of the cooker base and slide the collector into place.<PIC>", ["Manual30_38"]

"To install the sealing ring, place it over the sealing-ring rack and press it into place. Press down firmly to prevent puckering. When installed correctly, the sealing ring sits snugly behind the sealing-ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.<PIC>", ["Manual30_31"]

"To remove the silicone sealing ring, grip the edge of the silicone and pull the sealing ring out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing-ring rack. After removing it, inspect the steel rack to make sure it is centered, secured, and even in height all the way around the lid. Do not try to repair a deformed sealing-ring rack. The sealing ring is easiest to remove and install when it is wet and soapy.<PIC>", ["Manual30_30"]

"The sealing ring creates the air-tight seal between the pressure-cooking lid and the inner pot when the lid is closed. It must be installed before using the cooker, and only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.<PIC>Because silicone can absorb strong aromas and flavors, keeping extra sealing rings on hand helps prevent flavor transfer between different dishes.", ["Manual30_16"]

"The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe and releases steam from the top when the cooker depressurizes. It is required for pressure cooking and is part of the product's safety system.<PIC>Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve; keep exposed skin away from the steam path during release.", ["Manual30_15"]

"The quick release button controls the steam release valve, which determines whether pressure is held or released.<PIC>For quick release, press the button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position; steam ejects from the top of the steam release valve until pressure is released and the float valve drops.<PIC>Do not twist the quick release button beyond the small sealing/venting movement described in the manual; use the listed venting methods for safe depressurization.", ["Manual30_14", "multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01"]

"Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, means cooking stops gradually while the temperature inside the cooker drops and the cooker depressurizes naturally over time.<PIC>Use Natural Release for high-starch foods such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, and congee, or foods that expand during cooking such as beans and grains. Leave the cooker undisturbed until pressure has come down and it is safe to open the lid.", ["Manual30_9"]

"Robot anatomy overview: the vacuum body is shown as a round robot cleaner with the main top controls and front bumper area.<PIC>Top view labels include the iAdapt Localization Camera, handle, and bin release button.<PIC>The buttons and indicators include CLEAN, DOCK, SPOT, Wi-Fi indicator, troubleshooting indicator, battery indicator, full-bin indicator, and Dirt Detect indicator.<PIC>The bottom view identifies the debris extractors, extractor caps, dust bin, extractor frame and release tabs, side brush, charging contacts, and floor tracking sensor.<PIC>", ["Manual32_0", "Manual32_1", "Manual32_2", "Manual32_3"]

"The two primary modes are Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode on the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier. The device keeps the robot vacuum in areas you want cleaned and out of areas you want blocked.<PIC>Halo Mode, with the switch in the down position, creates a protective zone around items such as a bowl, vase, or other object the vacuum should avoid.<PIC>Virtual Wall Mode, with the switch in the up position, blocks openings up to 10 feet with an invisible cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum detects.<PIC>", ["Manual32_4", "Manual32_5", "Manual32_6"]

"To empty the vacuum bin: 1. Press the bin-release button to remove the bin.<PIC>2. Open the bin door to empty the bin.<PIC>If the full-bin indicator comes on during a cleaning job, pause the job, empty the bin, and continue cleaning. If the indicator is illuminated but the bin does not appear full, clean the full-bin sensors.", ["Manual32_8", "Manual32_9"]

"To clean the vacuum filter, remove the filter by grasping the yellow tab.<PIC>Shake off debris by tapping the filter against a trash container.<PIC>Reinsert the filter with the yellow tab facing up; the filter door will not close unless a filter is installed.", ["Manual32_10", "Manual32_11"]

"To clean the full-bin sensors, remove and empty the bin first.<PIC>Wipe the sensors on the vacuum with a clean, dry cloth; do not use liquid cleaner on the sensor openings.<PIC>Then wipe both the inner and outer sensor ports on the bin with a clean, dry cloth before reinstalling the bin.<PIC>", ["Manual32_12", "Manual32_13", "Manual32_14"]

"To clean the extractors, pinch the yellow extractor-frame release tabs, lift the extractor frame, and remove any obstructions.<PIC>Remove the extractors and yellow extractor caps, clear hair or debris from under the caps and around the metal axles, then reinstall the caps.<PIC>Remove hair and debris from the square and hexagonal plastic pegs on the other side of the extractors.<PIC>Clear the vacuum path, then reinstall the extractors by matching the color and shape of the extractor pegs to the icons on the cleaning head module.<PIC>", ["Manual32_19", "Manual32_20", "Manual32_21", "Manual32_22"]

"To clean the sensors and charging contacts, wipe the sensors with a clean, dry cloth. Do not spray cleaning solution directly onto sensors or sensor openings.<PIC>Then wipe the charging contacts on the vacuum and on the Home Base with a clean, dry cloth.<PIC>", ["Manual32_17", "Manual32_18"]

"Position the vacuum/Home Base in an open, uncluttered area: leave at least 1.5 feet on each side, at least 4 feet in front, at least 4 feet away from stairs, and at least 8 feet from virtual wall barriers. Keep the vacuum plugged in and place it where it has consistent coverage so it can receive information through the app.", []

"To clean the front caster wheel, pull firmly on the front wheel to remove it.<PIC>Remove debris from inside the wheel cavity. Spin the wheel by hand; if it does not rotate freely, remove the wheel from its housing, push firmly to remove the axle, and clear any debris or hair wrapped around it. Reinstall all parts when finished and make sure the wheel clicks back into place.", ["Manual32_15"]

"To clean the vacuum side brush, first use a coin or small screwdriver to remove the side-brush screw.<PIC>Lift off the side brush, then clear hair, thread, and debris from both the brush and the brush post. After cleaning, reinstall the brush on the post and tighten the screw so the brush is secure. Check that the brush can rotate normally before running the vacuum again. If hair is wrapped tightly around the post, remove it before reinstalling the brush so the motor is not loaded by debris.", ["Manual32_16"]

"The Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier controls where the robot vacuum is allowed to clean. It can block an opening or protect a specific object depending on the selected mode.<PIC>Halo Mode, with the switch down, creates a protective zone around items you do not want the vacuum to bump into or cross near.<PIC>Virtual Wall Mode, with the switch up, creates an invisible cone-shaped barrier across openings up to 10 feet wide.<PIC>", ["Manual32_4", "Manual32_5", "Manual32_6"]

"If the vacuum indicates a problem, listen for the two-tone distress sound and spoken message, and check whether the troubleshooting indicator is blinking. The buttons and indicators diagram shows where the troubleshooting indicator appears on the robot.<PIC>Use the app or online support for the matching error details and videos. For errors that may be cleared by rebooting, press and hold CLEAN for 10 seconds until all indicators illuminate, then release; an audible tone confirms a successful reboot. If you use scheduling, open the app after rebooting to confirm that the schedule remains intact.", ["Manual32_2"]

"T-rail mounting instructions: use the included hardware to mount the camera on a drop-ceiling T-rail. 1. Use the dashed lines on the mount-plate template as a guide to set the proper clip spacing.<PIC>2. Tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips and secure them with a 5/64-inch (2 mm) hex key.<PIC>3. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips using the mount-plate holes marked G.<PIC>4. Attach the T-rail clips to the T-rail by rotating and snapping them into place; the black foam pads should be slightly compressed after installation.<PIC>", ["Manual33_10", "Manual33_11", "Manual33_12", "Manual33_13"]

"To power the camera, remove the cable guard and route an Ethernet cable from an active port on an 802.3af PoE switch or PoE injector to the camera; the cable-routing opening/guard path is shown in the figure.<PIC>Power over Ethernet supplies power and network connectivity through the Ethernet connection, and the supported maximum cable length is 300 ft (100 m). After routing the cable, make sure it is seated securely before reinstalling any guard or continuing with mounting. Using a non-PoE port will not power the camera unless a proper PoE injector is installed in the line.", ["Manual33_3"]

"The brake lever stops the snowmobile by braking the entire drive system. Squeeze the brake lever toward the handlebar to stop; when the brake is operated, the brake light illuminates.<PIC>The parking brake button is used when parking the machine or starting the engine: squeeze the brake lever, push down the parking brake button while releasing the brake lever, and the brake stays applied. To release the parking brake, squeeze the brake lever again. Always set the parking brake before starting, but never run the machine with the parking brake applied because it can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking ability.<PIC>", ["Manual34_39", "Manual34_40"]

"Before Operating: read the Owner's Manual and all labels; become familiar with all operating controls and consult a dealer about any control or function you do not understand. Always perform the pre-operation checks before starting the engine, including throttle, steering and brake operation, and be sure the throttle lever moves freely and returns to idle. Apply the parking brake before starting, and do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine when starting, inspecting or adjusting it.<PIC>Handle fuel with care because it is highly flammable; never refuel when the engine is running or hot, use an approved fuel container, refuel outdoors, and wipe up spilled fuel immediately.<PIC>Wear protective clothing, including an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, snowmobile suit, boots, and gloves or mittens.<PIC>", ["Manual34_10", "Manual34_11", "Manual34_12"]

"STARTING THE ENGINE: check SAFETY INFORMATION, apply the parking brake, and make sure the engine stop switch is ON. Fully open the starter lever (choke): fully open for cold starting, half-open for warm-up, and close for warm starting. Manual Starting Model: turn the main switch ON, pull slowly on the recoil starter until it is engaged, then pull it briskly; warm up the engine until it runs smoothly.<PIC>Electric Starting Model: turn the main switch to START; after the engine starts, put the starter lever in the Half-open position and warm up the engine.<PIC>CAUTION: release the switch immediately after the engine starts. If it fails to start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, then try again; do not crank more than 10 seconds on one attempt.<PIC>", ["Manual34_116", "Manual34_117", "Manual34_118"]

"V-BELTHOLDER: keep a spare V-belt for emergency use by putting it into the holder provided. Loosen bolt 1 to remove the V-belt.<PIC>When installing the V-belt in the holder, tighten the bolt and make sure the fuel hose is not pinched and is in place.<PIC>The hose and belt placement is shown in the next illustration.<PIC>Install the V-belt securely in the holder so it is not damaged by the hot muffler.<PIC>", ["Manual34_51", "Manual34_52", "Manual34_53", "Manual34_55"]

"Throttle cable adjustment: first make sure the engine idle speed has been adjusted. 1. Loosen the adjuster locknut. 2. Turn the adjuster in or out until the proper throttle lever free play is achieved.<PIC>3. Tighten the locknut.<PIC>The completed adjustment view is shown in the next illustration.<PIC>", ["Manual34_148", "Manual34_149", "Manual34_150"]

"For the snowmobile steering system, the manual describes a pre-operation steering check rather than a riding maneuver. Before riding, check for excessive free play by pushing the handlebar up, down, back, and forth, then turning it slightly to the right and left. The steering should feel smooth and controlled; if excessive free play is noticed, consult a dealer before operating the snowmobile.<PIC>", ["Manual34_109"]

"TURNING: as you approach a curve, slow down and begin to turn the handlebars in the desired direction. Put your weight on the running board to the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn. Practice at slow speed in a large flat area with no obstacles; lean more as the turn gets sharper or is made at higher speeds. If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean more into the turn to regain balance, and if necessary gradually let off the throttle or steer to the outside of the turn.<PIC>", ["Manual34_127"]

"Riding uphill: practice first on gentle slopes and try more difficult climbs only after developing skill. As you approach a hill, accelerate before the climb, then reduce throttle to prevent track slippage. Keep your weight on the uphill side at all times; on straight climbs, lean forward as shown in the uphill riding posture diagram.<PIC>On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars; the standing uphill posture is shown in the second diagram.<PIC>Slow down near the crest and be ready for obstacles, sharp drops, people, or vehicles on the other side. If you cannot continue up the hill, do not spin the track: stop the engine, set the parking brake, pull the rear of the snowmobile around until it points downhill, restart, release the parking brake, and descend. Do not stand on the downhill side while turning the machine around.", ["Manual34_127", "Manual34_128"]

"When riding downhill, keep speed to a minimum. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while descending; this lets engine compression help slow the machine and prevents it from rolling freely downhill. Also apply the brake frequently with light pressure rather than relying on one hard brake application.<PIC>", ["Manual34_128"]

"CROSSING A SLOPE (SIDE HILL): side hills are not recommended for a beginner or novice snowmobiler. When crossing a slope, position your weight toward the uphill side to maintain balance. A recommended riding position is to kneel with the downhill knee on the seat and place the uphill foot on the running board so you can shift body weight as needed. If the snowmobile begins to slip sideways, steer in the direction of the slide if there are no obstacles; if it starts to tip, steer down the hill to regain balance.<PIC>", ["Manual34_129"]

"SPARK PLUG INSPECTION: the spark plug is an important engine component and its condition can indicate engine condition. Check the coloration around the center electrode; the ideal color is medium to light tan. If the color is distinctly different, consult your dealer rather than diagnosing the problem yourself. Periodically remove and inspect the spark plug because heat and deposits slowly break it down and erode it. Always use a spark plug with the proper reach.<PIC>Before installing any spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust to specification.<PIC>When installing the plug, clean the gasket surface, wipe grime from the threads, and torque the spark plug properly.", ["Manual34_138", "Manual34_139"]

"To clean the snowmobile for storage or maintenance, thoroughly clean the inside and outside of the machine to remove corrosive salts and acids. Use Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaner, to loosen mud, grease, and grime. Wash with mild soap, then rinse and dry the machine completely.", []

"To memorize channels by manual program, use the MEMORY/ERASE button: 1. Press the channel up/down buttons or number buttons to select the channel number you want to memorize or erase. 2. Press MEMORY/ERASE to select Memory or Erase; the on-screen display appears as shown.<PIC>You can also use MENU: open the menu, select Manual, choose Channel, and select the channel number.<PIC>Then choose Memory, set it On or Off, and press MENU repeatedly to exit.<PIC>", ["Manual35_9", "Manual35_10", "Manual35_11"]

"Common poor-reception causes include: IGNITION interference, where the picture may flutter, drift, or show black spots or horizontal streaks, usually from automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, or other appliances.<PIC>GHOSTS, where the TV signal follows both a direct path and a reflected path from tall buildings, hills, or other objects; changing the antenna direction or position may help.<PIC>SNOW, where weak fringe-area signals create small dots; a special antenna may be needed to improve the picture.<PIC>", ["television0_01", "television0_02", "television0_03"]

"To operate captions and on-screen text with the CAPTION button: press CAPTION, press it again to choose OFF, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2, then press ENTER to exit.<PIC>To use the MENU button: open MENU, select the Special menu, choose Caption, select Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2, then press MENU repeatedly to exit.<PIC>Closed captions convert program audio into written words on screen; text services can show full-screen information such as program lists, weather, stock exchange topics, or news.", ["Manual35_37", "Manual35_38"]

"For best reception, use an outdoor VHF/UHF antenna and inspect the antenna and cable before connection, because severely weathered antennas or cables can reduce signal quality. The diagram shows two common connection paths: a 75-ohm coaxial/CATV cable can connect to the TV rear 75-ohm antenna jack, while 300-ohm flat wire should be connected through a 300-to-75-ohm adapter.<PIC>If you are unsure which antenna is suitable, ask a service center about the outdoor antenna options available in your area.", ["Manual35_39"]

"To connect a DVD player, connect the component DVD inputs to Y, PB, PR (480i), and connect audio IN to the audio L/R ports.<PIC>Use the second connection diagram to confirm the component/audio wiring.<PIC>Then turn on the TV and press TV/VIDEO on the remote control or front panel to select COMPONENT. Try this after turning on the DVD set. The TV remembers the last mode it was set to, even if you turn it off.", ["Manual35_43", "Manual35_44"]

"Use the troubleshooting checklist by matching the symptom column to the checks marked in the table. Symptoms include no power, no picture or sound, poor picture with good sound, poor reception, lines in the picture, ghost or multiple images, remote control not working, and inability to tune the desired channel.<PIC>The key checks are: confirm the power cord is plugged in and the TV is turned on; check the remote-control batteries; try another channel for station trouble; check antenna connections; check for local interference; adjust the antenna or picture control; and program channels using AUTO PROGRAM when a desired channel cannot be tuned.", ["Manual35_45"]

"Follow these safety precautions: do not expose this product to rain or moisture; do not remove the cover or back because there are no user-serviceable parts inside; unplug the television before cleaning and use a damp cloth, not liquid or aerosol cleaners; operate it only from the power source indicated on the marking label; do not use it near water or place it on an unstable cart, stand, or table; keep cabinet ventilation openings unblocked; do not rest objects on the power cord, overload outlets, push objects into cabinet slots, or spill liquid; refer servicing to qualified personnel.", []

"The electric toothbrush has three intensity settings: high intensity with three lights, medium intensity with two lights, and low intensity with one light.<PIC>To choose the desired intensity, press the intensity indicator lights on the handle to cycle through the options. You can change intensity before, during, or after brushing, and the intensity can also be customized from the app.<PIC>", ["toothbrush0_06", "toothbrush0_07"]

"The electric toothbrush can activate or deactivate Adaptive Intensity, Pressure Sensor Feedback, Scrubbing Feedback, and the brush head replacement reminder. In the app, Adaptive Intensity and Scrubbing Feedback can be toggled directly. From the handle, place the handle on the charging stand, then press and hold the power button.<PIC>Hold up to 3 seconds for Adaptive Intensity, up to 5 seconds for the brush head replacement reminder, and up to 7 seconds for Pressure Sensor Feedback. White battery indication blinking 3 times with tones from low to high means the feature was activated; amber blinking 3 times with tones from high to low means it was deactivated. If Pressure Sensor Feedback is deactivated, Adaptive Intensity is also deactivated.", ["Manual37_13"]

"To charge the electric toothbrush in the travel case, plug the USB cord into the travel case and the USB wall adapter.<PIC>Plug the wall adapter into an electrical outlet, then place the toothbrush in the travel case.<PIC>Successful charging is indicated by two beeps and lights illuminating upward; while charging, the battery indicator blinks white.<PIC>Leave the travel case plugged in until the toothbrush is fully charged; when charging is complete, the battery light stops blinking and turns off.<PIC>Place the travel case on its side for better stability while charging.", ["Manual37_18", "Manual37_19", "Manual37_20", "Manual37_21"]
